title: The Second Sunday after the Epiphany
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## January 19, 2020 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

LITURGICAL CONTEXT
January 19, 2020 was the Second Sunday after the Epiphany in the Christian liturgical calendar. Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would have included 1 Samuel 3:1-10, Psalm 139:1-5 and 12-17, 1 Corinthians 6:12-20, and John 1:43-51—passages focused on calling, divine knowledge, and discipleship. This falls within the Epiphany season, a period emphasizing the revelation of Christ to the world and themes of vocation and light.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS
The Diocese of Colorado was operating under Bishop Marty Elfland, who had served since 2012. In early 2020, the Episcopal Church was still managing the aftermath of the 2018 General Convention's significant decisions on marriage and sexuality (Resolution A018), which opened all church offices including the episcopate to LGBTQ+ individuals. No major diocesan crises or bishop elections were imminent at this moment, though the national church remained in active conversations about implementation of these resolutions.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS
Denver was in mid-winter with typical January weather patterns. The previous week had seen Colorado legislative discussions beginning to form around the 2020 session, with education funding and healthcare access as primary topics. Saint John's Cathedral sits in the Capitol Hill neighborhood, an area experiencing ongoing gentrification pressures and serving as a hub for Denver's arts and activist communities. No major local catastrophes or events had dominated the preceding week's news cycle.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)
The dominant national story remained the impeachment trial of President Donald Trump in the Senate, which had begun just days earlier on January 16, 2020. The Democratic primary race was intensifying with Iowa caucuses scheduled for February 3, 2020—less than three weeks away. The Boeing 737 MAX crisis was ongoing, with investigations into two fatal crashes and the aircraft remaining grounded from service. COVID-19 had been identified in Wuhan, China but was not yet recognized as a major international threat in American consciousness; the first confirmed US case would be identified two days after this sermon on January 21, 2020.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS
The Iran-US crisis dominated headlines: Iran had launched ballistic missile strikes against US military bases in Iraq on January 8, 2020 in retaliation for the US assassination of Qasem Soleimani on January 3, marking a dangerous escalation in regional tensions. An unreported Ukrainian International Airlines flight had crashed near Tehran on January 8, killing all 176 people aboard—initially attributed to engine failure but later confirmed as shot down by Iranian air defense. Australia was still experiencing catastrophic bushfires, with fires having burned millions of acres and killed dozens of people throughout the preceding months.

CULTURAL MOMENT
American consciousness was polarized and anxious in January 2020—divided along partisan lines over impeachment, wary of escalating Middle East conflict, and for the most part unaware of the pandemic that would emerge within days. The mood was one of political intensity and cultural fragmentation. Locally in Denver, the Denver Broncos' disappointing 2019 season (7-9 record) had concluded, and the city's sports attention was turning toward the Colorado Avalanche (then in a competitive NHL season) and anticipation of spring training baseball. Cultural escapism was strong; the film "1917" and "Parasite" were commanding box office and critical attention.
